essence: Media is a hits-and-library business: expensive, risky content where a few hits pay for many misses, and a deep back-catalogue (library) is the durable asset.
Two monetisation models fight it out: subscriptions (predictable, retention-driven) and advertising (scale x engagement) - increasingly blended in ad-supported tiers.
Content is both the cost and the moat: huge upfront spend, near-zero marginal distribution cost, so scale and engagement decide profitability.
Attention is the scarce resource; the battle is for time spent, against every other form of entertainment.
It's shifting from owning distribution (channels/theatres) to owning the audience relationship and data (streaming).

definition.oneLiner: Companies that create, package and distribute content - video, audio, games, news - monetised through subscriptions, advertising, or transactions.
definition.jobToBeDone: Entertain and inform - capture people's attention and time with content they value enough to pay for or watch ads around.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools sit in owned IP/franchises and large engaged audiences; libraries (back-catalogue) generate annuity-like returns while one-off content is high-risk; aggregation/distribution platforms capture value by owning the customer relationship and data.
valueChain.powerShift: Value has shifted from distribution control (channels, theatres, cable) -> streaming platforms that own the audience -> AI-personalised content & creation, with advertising returning via ad-supported streaming tiers.

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per subscriber (ARPU) for subscription; revenue per user-hour / impression for ads
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Subscribers x ARPU. Revenue grows with subscriber additions, ARPU (price + ad tiers), engagement, and content-driven retention.

costStructure.shape: Dominated by content spend (huge, upfront, partly fixed) amortised over time; distribution is near-zero marginal cost, so the question is whether content drives enough engagement and retention to earn back its cost.
costStructure.leverage: Big operating leverage - the same content serves millions at near-zero extra cost - but only if it attracts and retains a large audience; under-watched content is sunk cost.

moats: Scale to fund content & spread cost
competitionAndMoats.concentration: Fragmenting attention across many streamers ('streaming wars'), driving consolidation and bundling; a few global giants plus strong regional players.

structuralForces.regulation: Moderate - content/censorship rules, OTT self-regulation, copyright/IP law, local-content quotas, and data privacy; varies sharply by geography.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Advertising is cyclical (ad budgets fall in downturns); subscriptions are more resilient but churn in tight times; content spend cycles with competition.
structuralForces.substitution: Intense - every entertainment option competes for attention: social media, gaming, short-form video (YouTube/Reels), piracy; the real competitor is 'time spent anywhere'.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Top talent and IP owners command huge power (and cost); distribution platforms/app stores take a cut; consumers churn easily (low switching cost, multi-subscribe).

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium-to-high and accelerating - AI is reshaping recommendation, production, and increasingly content creation itself.
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI slashes production/localisation costs and supercharges personalisation (retention), but generative content raises IP, quality and trust questions and could flood the market, pressuring premium-content economics.

ranges: Content as % of revenue: very high for streamers
Streaming ARPU: far higher in developed markets than India
Hit rate: a minority of titles drive most engagement
AVOD CPMs: vary by market & targeting
Library/IP can be ~half a studio's value
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

valueLevers: Improve content ROI (spend on what drives engagement/retention)
Monetise IP across windows (licensing, franchises, merch)
Bundle & partner to lower CAC and churn
Cut production cost via AI / localisation

keyTerms.SVOD / AVOD / FAST: Subscription / Advertising / Free Ad-Supported Streaming TV video models.
keyTerms.Content amortisation: Spreading a title's production cost over its useful life.
keyTerms.Library: The back-catalogue of owned content - an annuity-like asset.
keyTerms.IP / franchise: Owned intellectual property re-monetisable across formats.
keyTerms.Engagement: Time spent / frequency - the leading indicator of retention.
keyTerms.Windowing: Releasing content across channels/times to maximise revenue.

questionsToAskFirst: Subscription, ad, or hybrid - and where's the money?
What's churn, and what content drives retention?
Content spend vs engagement - is content ROI positive?
How deep/valuable is the owned IP / library?
Engagement trend - is attention growing or leaking?
Distribution - owned platform or dependent on others?
rookieTraps: Spending on content that doesn't move engagement/retention
Chasing subscriber growth while churn leaks
Ignoring the library/IP as a durable asset
Underrating free/short-form video as the real competitor for attention

whatsUniqueHere: Media is a portfolio business of expensive bets: most content loses money, a few hits pay for everything, and the back-catalogue quietly compounds like an annuity. Because distribution is now nearly free, the scarce resources are attention and great content - and the durable winners own IP and the audience relationship, not just the pipes. AI now threatens to make content abundant and cheap, which paradoxically makes owned franchises and trusted brands even more valuable.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: A huge, price-sensitive, mobile-first market: streaming led by JioHotstar (the merged Disney+Hotstar / JioCinema giant), Netflix and Amazon Prime; a massive film industry (Bollywood + powerful regional cinema), dominant broadcast TV, booming short-form video and gaming, and a streaming-led music market.
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Software / SaaS - subscription, churn, retention economics

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India: the JioHotstar mega-merger created a dominant streaming + sports platform, intensifying the battle with Netflix and Prime; ad-supported and mobile-only low-price tiers are key to scaling in a low-ARPU market; regional content and live cricket are decisive; AI dubbing/localisation is expanding reach.
keyTrends: Ad-supported & mobile low-price tiers for scale
Short-form video & creator economy pulling attention
